person.name: Fan Yujie (Nichole)
person.affiliation: School of Social Sciences, Nanyang Technological University
person.bio: I am a PhD candidate in Economics at Nanyang Technological University, supervised by Associate Professor Wang Wenjie and co-supervised by Professor Yohanes Eko Riyanto. My research interests sit at the intersection of experimental economics, machine learning applications, and human-AI interaction, with ongoing work on matching in two-sided markets and human-AI interaction in teams. I am also actively involved in undergraduate teaching in economics and econometrics.
person.bioHtml: I am a PhD candidate in Economics at Nanyang Technological University, supervised by Associate Professor Wang Wenjie and co-supervised by Professor Yohanes Eko Riyanto. My research interests sit at the intersection of experimental economics, machine learning applications, and human-AI interaction, with ongoing work on matching in two-sided markets and human-AI interaction in teams. I am also actively involved in undergraduate teaching in economics and econometrics.

interests.title: Experimental Economics
interests.description: Designing and analyzing behavioral mechanisms in controlled economic settings, with attention to incentives, matching, and strategic interaction.

interests.title: Machine Learning Applications
interests.description: Using computational tools to support empirical economic research, prediction, classification, and heterogeneity analysis.

interests.title: Human-AI Interaction
interests.description: Studying how AI systems shape collaboration, decision-making, productivity, and team dynamics.

projects.title: AI in Teamwork
projects.description: This project investigates how AI integration modulates team dynamics and performance across both flat (leaderless) and hierarchical (leader-led) organizational structures. In leaderless settings, the research evaluates the impact of AI on collective output and individual member confidence, specifically examining whether the introduction of AI obscures the star effect typically driven by consistently high-performing individuals. Within the leadership framework, the study examines how the sophistication of AI assistance, ranging from standard AI and certainty AI to personalized AI, interacts with the method of leader selection. By comparing teams with self-promoted versus randomly assigned leaders, the project analyzes how leadership legitimacy and AI modality jointly influence team outcomes and decision-making efficacy.

publications.title: Health Inequality and Health Types: Evidence from China
publications.authors: Yujie Fan, Zhuoru Ma, Akshar Saxena, Wenjie Wang
publications.abstract: China's rapidly aging population faces substantial health inequality, yet we lack systematic evidence on how individual health evolves differently across midlife and into old age. Understanding these trajectories matters for pension sustainability, healthcare demand, and targeting of social protection. Using data from the China Health and Retirement Longitudinal Study (CHARLS), we apply K-means clustering to identify distinct health trajectory types based on frailty index evolution over ten years. We find five distinct patterns between ages 46-56, which differ sharply in both initial health and deterioration rates. Individuals with similar baseline health can follow diverging paths - some remain stable while others decline rapidly. By age 66-76, these patterns converge into three types, suggesting health trajectories become more homogeneous with age. Trajectory types identified from waves 1-4 predict wave 5 health outcomes substantially better than demographic characteristics alone, indicating persistent unobserved heterogeneity in health dynamics. We examine whether such health type heterogeneity exists in the Chinese context, where rapid economic development and healthcare expansion create a unique environment for studying health dynamics. China's experience may offer insights for other middle-income countries undergoing similar transitions.
